attributes.stem:
An 80-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department after an unwitnessed fall at home. She normally mobilises with a walker due to chronic osteoarthritis of her hips and knees. She was conscious throughout the event, and fell as her foot became caught on the edge of a rug. She has previously sustained fractures of her wrist, femur, and pelvic rami from falls. She has a history of hypertension, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, mild cognitive impairment, asthma, and achalasia. Her current medications are shown below:

  • Prednisolone 37.5mg daily - 5 days course for asthma exacerbations

  • Salbutamol 200mcg BD

  • Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/25mcg BD

  • Ramipril 2.5mg daily

  • Diltiazem CR 180mg daily

  • Donepezil 10mg daily

On examination, her supine blood pressure is 140/80mmHg, standing blood pressure 89/60mmHg, pulse rate 58/min, respiratory rate 18/min, and SpO2 95% on room air. There is a skin tear over her left forearm, but no other injury evident. ECG shows sinus rhythm with no evidence of recent ischaemia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate approach to her medication management?
attributes.explanation:
Although the primary cause of this patient's fall appears to be mechanical on this occasion, the finding of orthostatic hypotension warrants attention. Orthostatic hypotension is an important risk factor for falls in the older population. A postural drop of >20mmHg systolic or >10mmHg diastolic is considered significant, and occurs when the normal haemodynamic response to a posture change cannot be mounted. Mechanisms include autonomic dysfunction, intravascular depletion, decreased venous return, and/or reduced cardiac responsiveness. Orthostatic hypotension is linked to both the treatment of hypertension itself and the various different groups of anti-hypertensive drugs.

Findings of orthostatic hypotension in this case warrants for a review of her hypertensive medications. It is likely that combination of ACE-inhibitor (ramipril) and calcium-channel blocker (diltiazem) contribute to her severe orthostatic hypotension. Due to her history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HfRf), the most appropriate step in this case is to cease diltiazem (calcium channel blocker). ACE-inhibitor has direct mortality benefit for patients with HfRf, therefore ceasing ramipril or reducing its dose is not an appropriate management plan. In addition, It is important to review the patient closely and monitor her blood pressure more regularly when changes to her anti-hypertensive medications are made.

No changes to her medications is not the most appropriate response. As mentioned, orthostatic hypotension is an important risk factors for falls and is something that should be thoroughly investigated and addressed, particularly in a patient who has recently had a fall.

Increase donepezil is not the most appropriate response. Donepezil is associated with an increased risk of orthostatic hypotension and bradycardia may be potentiated by the use of donepezil, thus this should not be increased. Furthermore, any changes to this medication should be very considered due to the impact they have on her quality of life and her ability to engage in her activities of daily living (ADLs).
learningPoints: A postural drop of >20mmHg systolic or >10mmHg diastolic is considered significant, and occurs when the normal haemodynamic response to a posture change cannot be mounted.
references: Juraschek SP, Appel LJ, Miller ER, et al. Hypertension Treatment Effects on Orthostatic Hypotension and its Relationship with Cardiovascular Disease: Results from the AASK Trial. Hypertension 2018; 72:986-93.

Bhanu C, Nimmons D, Petersen I, Orlu M, Davis D, Hussain H, Magammanage S, Walters K. Drug-induced orthostatic hypotension: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials. PLoS medicine. 2021 Nov 9;18(11):e1003821.

Van Vark LC, Bertrand M, Akkerhuis KM, Brugts JJ, Fox K, Mourad JJ, Boersma E. Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors reduce mortality in hypertension: a meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials of renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system inhibitors involving 158 998 patients. European heart journal. 2012 Aug 1;33(16):2088-97.

Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines [published correction appears in Circulation. 2022 May 3;145(18):e1033. doi: 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001073] [published correction appears in Circulation. 2022 Sep 27;146(13):e185. doi: 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001097] [published correction appears in Circulation. 2023 Apr 4;147(14):e674. doi: 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001142]. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895-e1032. doi:10.1161/CIR.0000000000001063
